source_metadata.title_raw: Michael C. Ghizzoni - A-18-134 - October 11, 2018 - Santa Barbara

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 11, 2018

Michael C. Ghizzoni
County Counsel
County of Santa Barbara
105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 201
Santa Barbara, CA 93101

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-18-134

Dear Mr. Ghizzoni:

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara County
Supervisor Peter Adam regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090. Additionally, please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should
change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

        May Supervisor Adam take part in the Board of Supervisors decisions regarding a project to
reestablish oil and gas production in a long-dormant portion of the Cat Canyon Oil Fields, given
that Supervisor Adam has an interest in property adjacent to the project?

CONCLUSION

        No. Based on the information provided, Board decisions regarding the project will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor Adam's interests.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

        Peter Adam is a Santa Barbara County Supervisor for the Fourth Supervisorial District.
Supervisor Adam is also a member of PDK Farming LLC ("PDK"), has an ownership interest of
more than $2,000 and greater than 10-percent, and receives annual income exceeding $500.

_____________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-134
Page No. 2

Cat Canyon Oil Fields ("Cat Canyon") is a California State-designated oil field with
boundaries defined by the California Department of Conservation, Division of Oil, Gas and
Geothermal Resources ("DOGGR"). Cat Canyon covers over 26,440 acres and includes nearly
1,600 active and idle oil wells.

Aera Energy, LLC ("Aera") applied to the County Planning and Development Department
for an Oil and Drilling Production Plan, Development Plan, and a Vesting Tentative Tract Map,
which would allow Aera to reestablish oil and gas production operations on the Aera-owned
property in Cat Canyon (the "Aera Project").

The Aera Project site consists of 2,107.8 acres and is located in the eastern area of Cat
Canyon, which was used for oil production purposes for more than 100 years.² However,
production activities at the site were shut down in 1989, at which time, the wells were abandoned
and nearly all facilities were removed by 2002.³ All of the oil wells at the Aera Project site are
currently listed as plugged and abandoned, with the exception of four non-oil producing reservoir
sampling and test wells.⁴ The Aera Project application involves numerous properties located along
Cat Canyon Road and Long Canyon Road, near the communities of Garey and Sisquoc.

The Aera Project includes the following components:

• Construction and restoration of approximately 72 well pad locations.
• Development and operation of up to 296 wells, including oil/gas production wells, steam
injection wells, observation wells, non-potable water production wells, water injection
wells, and fresh groundwater wells. The Area Project will not use hydraulic fracturing.
• Construction and restoration of over nine miles of field access roads.
• Construction of new processing facilities, including: a production group station for bulk
separation of produced gas and liquids; a central processing facility for oil cleaning,
water cleaning, water softening, oil storage, and oil sales; and a steam generation site
with up to six once-through steam generators.
• Construction of new field systems, including: production gathering and water
distribution network; steam distribution network; electrical power distribution; and
supervisory control and data acquisition networks.
• Construction of a 3,000-barrel tank and water distribution pipelines for ancillary
purposes, including fire protection, lavatories and showers, equipment cleaning, dust
control, irrigation, and possibly for drinking water. No fresh water would be used to
steam; non-potable brackish water would be used as the primary source of water for
steam generation to minimize the use of potable groundwater.
• Construction of support facilities, including an office building, a multipurpose building,
a warehouse and maintenance building, a control building, and an onsite septic system.

² See http://www.sbcountyplanning.org/energy/projects/EastCatCanyon/PermitApplication/Aera-VI.asp

³ Energy Div., County of Santa Barbara Planning and Development, Aera East Cat Canyon Oil Field
Redevelopment Project Permit Application Package (Updated Apr. 26, 2016) Introduction and Project Overview, p. 1-
10.

⁴ Ibid.

File No. A-18-134
Page No. 3

• Construction of a new approximately 14-mile, 8-inch natural gas pipeline and associated
facilities, which include above-ground and underground valves and a metering station.
• Construction of a new, approximately 0.3-mile 115 kilovolt power line to interconnect
Pacific Gas and Electric Company's Sisquoc-Santa Ynez power line to a new Aera-
owned substation, located within the processing facility.
• Importation of light crude for blending from Aera's Belridge Producing Complex near
Bakersfield (134 miles), and exportation of produced blended crude oil back to Aera's
Belridge complex.
• A permanent conservation easement, located in an area east of Long Canyon Road,
where no surface oil production activities are proposed; the easement will be used to
mitigate the unavoidable Aera Project impacts and to provide conservation, education,
and recreational opportunities for the community.

PDK owns a parcel adjacent to Cat Canyon that shares a property line with the Aera-owned
property for approximately 4.364 feet (the "Parcel"). For many years, the Parcel has been used to
graze cattle, but nearly 80 acres nearest to Cat Canyon are currently being converted for growing
wine grapes. The Parcel is not engaged in oil or gas drilling or production, and is separated from
Cat Canyon by steep slopes that limit views from the Parcel of current and proposed Cat Canyon
facilities.

The equipment and parts needed to build the Aera Project will be trucked into Cat Canyon
via Highway 101 and Clark Avenue/Dominion Road, and are not anticipated to affect traffic at the
Parcel and no new public roads are proposed as part of the Aera Project. Of the 72 well pad
locations proposed, currently none are anticipated to be visible from the Parcel. The Parcel's
groundwater wells are not proposed to be affected.

The County Land Use and Development Code designates oil and gas extraction as an
allowed zoning use for the Aera Project site and no change in existing land use designation and/or
zone district is proposed. The Parcel and two of Aera's adjacent parcels are currently enrolled in
Agricultural Preserve Contracts.

Land uses surrounding the Aera Project site include: oil and gas production and grazing to
the north, south, east, and west, and agriculture further to the south and west. The Aera Project site
sits in a canyon, surrounded by rolling hills and some steeper slopes. In addition to oil production
operations, the Aera Project property is used for cattle grazing.

An additional project is also being proposed on the western side of Cat Canyon by ERG
Operating Company, LLC ("ERG"), for which Supervisor Adam sought advice in January 2018.
(See Ghizzoni Advice Letter, No. A-18-008.) ERG applied for a Petroleum Production Plan and a
companion Development Plan for their oil operations located in the western area of Cat Canyon
(collectively the "ERG Project"). The ERG Project would increase the number of oil and natural
gas wells and related infrastructure on property that ERG currently owns or leases in Cat Canyon.

All proposed ERG Project facilities would be located within an approximately 75-acre
portion of ERG's 8,054-acre property holdings. Of the additional well pads proposed for the ERG
Project (233 new steam injection wells, 189 of which are proposed to be located on existing well
pads), none would be visible from the Parcel, and the nearest new well pad would be located at

File No. A-18-134
Page No. 4

approximately 2,400-feet away from the Parcel. Water for steaming will come from brine water
produced and treated by ERG's Cat Canyon field operations. The ERG Project area is located in a
canyon, surrounded by rolling hills and some steeper slopes. The Parcel is separated from Cat
Canyon by steep slopes that limit the views from the Parcel of the current Cat Canyon facilities and
the ERG Project additions.

In addition to the Ara and ERG Projects, in an e-mail from July 23, 2018, you indicated
that another oil and gas production project was in development for PetroRock, LLC. PetroRock,
LLC proposed to reactivate oil production on 28 acres of its 710-acre holdings in the north central
portion of Cat Canyon, which includes 231 new oil, injection, and water wells located on 29 well
pads, a centralized tank battery, pipelines, and ancillary equipment (the "PetroRock Project").5

The Planning Commission has not yet reviewed the Aera Project, but is anticipated to
review and consider the following decisions, which may be appealed de novo by the Board of
Supervisors: 1) approval of the East Cat Canyon Oil Drilling and Production Plan and Development
Plan with up to 296 wells, including oil/gas production wells, steam injection wells, observation
wells, non-potable water production wells, water injection wells, and fresh groundwater wells, and
related infrastructure; 2) approval of a Vesting Tentative Tract Map to reconfigure 14 lots into 12
lots; and 3) certification of the environmental impact report pursuant to the California
Environmental Quality Act.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation
18700(a).) Specific to the provided facts, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict
may arise, including:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).6

• An interest in real property in which the officer has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

___________________________
5 See http://sbcountyplanning.org/energy/projects/PetroRockUCCB.asp

6 "For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-
percent interest or greater."

File No. A-18-134
Page No. 5

• An interest in your personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.7

As implicated by the facts, Supervisor Adam has interests in PDK as a business entity as
well as a source of income, and a property interest in the Parcel owned by PDK.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision.8 For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision at
issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation
18701(b).) Supervisor Adam's interests are not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue.

Real Property

Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has a
financial interest is material. Pertinent to the provided facts, the financial effect will be material if
the decision:

"(7) Would change the development potential of the parcel of real property;"

[¶]…[¶]

"(9) Would change the highest and best use of the parcel of real property in
which the official has a financial interest;"

"(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air
quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of
the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;"

[¶]…[¶]

"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was

7 An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members is not
considered separately if the decision also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation
18702.5(c)). Because the decisions at issue also affect your business and real property interests, we do not further
analyze your personal finances or those of your immediate family.

8 An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named
party in, or subject of, the decision. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
financial interest, or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).
(Regulation 18701(a).)

File No. A-18-134
Page No. 6

of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market
value of the official's property."

In this case, unlike the ERG Project, oil and gas operations at the Aera Project site ceased in
1989, most facilities were removed in 2002, and Aera's 180 original wells were listed as plugged
and abandoned. The Aera Project site contemplates large-scale infrastructure renovations and
improvements, as well as the installation of 296 new wells and 72 new well pads, which is roughly
the same amount of planned new well pads contemplated by the ERG and PetroRock Projects
combined. Additionally, unlike the ERG Project, which limited the Project site to an approximately
73-acre portion of ERG's 8,054-acre property holdings, the Aera Project requires the complete
overhaul and redevelopment of its entire 2,100-acre holdings.

The substantial and expansive revitalization and redevelopment project shares a property
line with the Parcel for approximately 4,364 feet, buttressing the property. Given the scope and
scale associated with a complete overhaul of the previously dormant Aera Project site, the
unavoidable impacts and environmental consequences associated with the Aera Project implicate
long-scheme land use and development concerns for surrounding properties, including the Parcel.
Therefore, decisions related to the Aera Project are likely to have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Supervisor Adam's interest in the Parcel.⁹

Business Entity and Source of Income

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a financial effect is material if a prudent person with
sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect
would contribute to a change in the value of a privately-held business entity.¹⁰

In this case, PDK utilizes the Parcel for commercial purposes and, as stated above, the
cumulative impacts from the Aera Project on surrounding properties appear to be significant.
Specifically, a reasonable inference can be made that given the environmental consequences
associated with oil and gas production, the revitalization of a once-dormant business (including new
infrastructure, supporting staff, and daily operations), combined with potential land use and
development impacts, the revival of a massive oil and gas production enterprise will likely result
in reasonably foreseeable financial effects on businesses and properties surrounding the project site,
including the Parcel upon which PDK operates. Therefore, decisions related to the Aera Project
appear to have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on PDK's value.

_______________
⁹ We concluded in our previous letter, Ghizzoni Advice Letter, No. A-18-008, that the ERG project would not
have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on Supervisor Adam's financial interests. That advice was based on the
characteristics specific to the ERG project, which is distinguished by the Aera project's scope and scale.

¹⁰ Under Regulation 18702.3, the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the
same as for an interest in the business entity itself. Therefore, we do not analyze Supervisor Adam's source of income
interest.

File No. A-18-134
Page No. 7

Accordingly, Supervisor Adam has a disqualifying conflict of interest in Board decisions
regarding the Project as such decisions will likely have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on
Supervisor Adam's interests.11

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Sara K. Puricelli
Counsel, Legal Division

SKP:gal

______________________________________________________________________________

11 Please note that when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 has a conflict of
interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the
item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest
on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion
and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

cumulative impacts from the Aera Project on surrounding properties appear to be significant. 
Specifically, a reasonable inference can be made that given the environmental consequences 
associated with oil and gas production, the revitalization of a once-dormant business (including new 
infrastructure, supporting staff, and daily operations), combined with potential land use and 
developmental impacts, the revival of a massive oil and gas production enterprise will likely result 
in reasonably foreseeable financial effects on businesses and properties surrounding the project site, 
including the Parcel upon which PDK operates. Therefore, decisions related to the Aera Project 
appear to have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on PDK's value. 
9 We concluded in our previous letter, Ghizzoni Advice Letter, No. A-18-008, that the ERG project would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on Supervisor Adam's financial interests. That advice was based on the 
characteristics specific to the ERG project, which is distinguished by the Aera project's scope and scale. 
' 10 Under Regulation 18702.3, the materiality standard for a source of income that is a business entity is the 
same as for an interest in the business entity itself. Therefore, we do not analyze Supervisor Adam's source of income 
interest.  
Accordingly, Supervisor Adam_ has a disqualifying conflict of interest in Board decisions 
regarding the Project as such decisions will likely have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on 
Supervisor Adam's interests. 11

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Supervisor Adam take part in the Board of Supervisors decisions regarding a project to 
reestablish oil and gas production in a long-dormant portion of the Cat Canyon Oil Fields, given 
that Supervisor Adam has an interest in property adjacent to the project?

CONCLUSION: No. Based on the information provided, Board decisions regarding the project will have a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor Adam's interests. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
Peter Adam is a Santa Barbara County Supervisor for the Fourth Supervisorial District. 
Supervisor Adam is also a member of PDK Farming LLC ("PDK"), has an ownership interest of 
more than $2,000 and greater than 10-percent, and receives annual income exceeding $500.Cat Canyon Oil Fields ("Cat Canyon") is a California State-designated oil field with 
boundaries defined by the California Department of Conservation, Division of Oil, Gas and 
Geothermal Resources ("DOGGR"). Cat Canyon covers over 26,440 acres and includes nearly 
1,600 active and idle oil wells. 
Aera Energy, LLC ("Aera") applied to the County Planning and Development Department 
for an Oil and Drilling Production Plan, Development Plan, and a Vesting Tentative Tract Map, 
which would allow Aera to reestablish oil and gas production operations on the Aera-owned 
property in Cat Canyon (the "Aera Project"). 
The Aera Project site consists of 2,107.8 acres and is located in the eastern area of Cat 
Canyon, which was used for oil production purposes for more than 100 years.2 However, 
production activities at the site were shut down in 1989, at which time, the wells were abandoned 
and nearly all facilities were removed by 2002.3 All of the oil wells at the Aera Project site are 
currently listed as plugged and abandoned, with the exception of four non-oil producing reservoir 
sampling and test wells.4 The Aera Project application involves numerous properties located along 
Cat Canyon Road and Long Canyon Road, near the communities of Garey and Sisquoc. 
The Aera Project includes the following components: 
• 
Construction and restoration of approximately 72 well pad locations. 
• 
Development and operation ofup to 296 wells, including oil/gas production wells, steam 
injection wells, observation wells, non-potable water production wells, water injection 
wells, and fresh groundwater wells. The Area Project will not use hydraulic fracturing. 
• 
Construction and restoration of over nine miles of field access roads. 
• 
Construction of new processing facilities, including: a production group station for bulk 
separation of produced gas and liquids; a central processing facility for oil cleaning, 
water cleaning, water softening, oil storage, and oil sales; and a steam generation site 
with up to six once-through steam generators. 
• 
Construction of new field systems, including: production gathering and water 
distribution network; steam distribution network; electrical power distribution; and 
supervisory control and data acquisition networks. 
• 
Construction of a 3,000-barrel tank and water distribution pipelines for ancillary 
purposes, including fire protection, lavatories and showers, equipment cleaning, dust 
control, irrigation, and possibly for drinking water. No fresh water would be used to 
steam; non-potable brackish water would be used as the primary source of water for 
steam generation to minimize the use of potable groundwater. 
• 
Construction of support facilities, including an office building, a multipurpose building, 
a warehouse and maintenance building, a control building, and an onsite septic system. 
2 See http://www.sbcountyplann ing. org/energy/pro j ects/EastCatCanyon/Perm itApp Ii cation/ A era-VI .asp 
3 Energy Div., County of Santa Barbara Planning and Development, Aera East Cat Canyon Oil Field 
Redevelopment Project Permit Application Package (Updated Apr. 26, 2016) Introduction and Project Overview, p. 1-
10. 
4 Ibid.  
• 
Construction of a new approximately 14-mile, 8-inch natural gas pipeline and associated 
facilities, which include above-ground and underground valves and a metering station. 
• 
Construction of a new, approximately 0.3-mile 115 kilovolt power line to interconnect 
Pacific Gas and Electric Company's Sisquoc-Santa Ynez power line to a new Aera-
owned substation, located within the processing facility. 
• 
Importation of light crude for blending from Aera's Belridge Producing Complex near 
Bakersfield (134 miles), and exportation of produced blended crude oil back to Aera's 
Belridge complex. 
• 
A permanent conservation easement, located in an area east of Long Canyon Road, 
where no surface oil production activities are proposed; the easement will be used to 
mitigate the unavoidable Aera Project impacts and to provide conservation, education, 
and recreational opportunities for the community. 
PDK owns a parcel adjacent to Cat Canyon that shares a property line with the Aera-owned 
property for approximately 4,364 feet (the "Parcel"). For many years, the.Parcel has been used to 
graze cattle, but nearly 80 acres nearest to Cat Canyon are currently being converted for growing 
wine grapes. The Parcel is not engaged in oil or gas drilling or production, and is separated from 
Cat Canyon by steep slopes that, limit views from the Parcel of current and proposed Cat Canyon 
facilities. 
The equipment and parts needed to build the Aera Project will be trucked into Cat Canyon 
via Highway 101 and Clark A venue/Dominion Road, and are not anticipated to affect traffic at the 
Parcel and no new public roads are proposed as part of the Aera Project. Of the 72 well pad 
locations proposed, currently none are anticipated to be visible from the Parcel. The Parcel's 
groundwater wells are not proposed to be affected. 
The County Land Use and Development Code designates oil and gas extraction as an 
. 
allowed zoning use for the Aera Project site and no change in existing land use designation and/or 
zone district is proposed. The Parcel and two of Aera's adjacent parcels are currently enrolled in 
Agricultural Preserve Contracts. 
Land uses surrounding the Aera Project site include: oil and gas production and grazing to 
the north, south, east, and west, and agriculture further to the south and west. The Aera Project site 
sits in a canyon, surrounded by rolling hills and some steeper slopes. In addition to oil production 
operations, the A era Project property is used for cattle grazing. 
An additional project is also being proposed on the western side of Cat Canyon by ERG 
Operating Company, LLC ("ERG"), for which Supervisor Adam sought advice in January 2018. 
(See Ghizzoni Advice Letter, No. A-18-008.) ERG applied for a Petroleum Production Plan and a 
companion Development Plan for their oil operations located in the western area of Cat Canyon 
(collectively the "ERG Project"). The ERG Project would increase the number of oil and natural 
gas wells and related infrastructure on property that ERG currently owns or leases in Cat Canyon. 
All proposed ERG Project facilities would be located within an approximately 75-acre 
portion ofERG's 8,054-acre property holdings. Of the additional well pads proposed for the ERG 
Project (233 new steam injection wells, 189 of which are proposed to be located on existing well 
pads), none would be visible from the Parcel, and the nearest new well pad would be located  
approximately 2,400-feet away from the Parcel. Water for steaming will come from brine water 
produced and treated by ERG's Cat Canyon field operations. The ERG Project area is located in a 
canyon, surrounded by rolling hills and some steeper slopes. The Parcel is separated from Cat 
Canyon by steep slopes that limit the views from the Parcel of the current Cat Canyon facilities and 
the ERG Project additions. 
In addition to the Aera and ERG Projects, in an e-mail from July 23, 2018, you indicated 
that another oil and gas production project was in development for PetroRock, LLC. PetroRock, 
LLC proposed to reactivate oil production on 28 acres of its 710-acre holdings in the north central 
portion of Cat Canyon, which includes 231 new oil, injection, and water wells located on 29 well 
pads, a centralized tank battery, pipelines, and ancillary equipment (the "PetroRock Project").5 
The Planning Commission has not yet reviewed the Aera Project, but is anticipated to 
review and consider the following decisions, which may be appealed de novo by the Board of 
Supervisors: 1) approval of the East Cat Canyon Oil Drilling and Production Plan and Development 
Plan with up to 296 wells, including oil/gas production wells, steam injection wells, observation 
wells, non-potable water production wells, water injection wells, and fresh groundwater wells, and 
related infrastructure; 2) approval of a Vesting Tentative Tract Map to reconfigure 14 lots into 12 
lots; and 3) certification of the environmental impact report pursuant to the California 
Environmental Quality Act.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 October 11, 2018 Michael C. Ghizzoni County Counsel County of Santa Barbara 105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 201 Santa Barbara, CA 93101 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-18-134 Dear Mr. Ghizzoni: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Santa Barbara County Supervisor Peter Adam regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Additionally, please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION May Supervisor Adam take part in the Board of Supervisors decisions regarding a project to reestablish oil and gas production in a long-dormant portion of the Cat Canyon Oil Fields, given that Supervisor Adam has an interest in property adjacent to the project? CONCLUSION No. Based on the information provided, Board decisions regarding the project will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor Adam's interests. FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR Peter Adam is a Santa Barbara County Supervisor for the Fourth Supervisorial District. Supervisor Adam is also a member of PDK Farming LLC ("PDK"), has an ownership interest of more than $2,000 and greater than 10-percent, and receives annual income exceeding $500. _____________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. Cat Canyon covers over 26,440 acres and includes nearly 1,600 active and idle oil wells. The Aera Project site consists of 2,107.8 acres and is located in the eastern area of Cat Canyon, which was used for oil production purposes for more than 100 years.² However, production activities at the site were shut down
